title: Two-sample t interval for the difference of means (calculator-active)
description: Practice calculating a two-sample t interval to estimate the difference between population means.
topic_path: Confidence intervals for the difference of two means
Two-sample t interval for the difference of means (calculator-active)

assessment_items.question_content:
Shelby heard that light roast coffee at her favorite shop has a higher caffeine content than the shop's dark roast coffee. She obtains a random sample of large coffees of both types and measures the amount of caffeine in each coffee. Here are summary statistics for her samples:

 | Light roast | Dark roast
- | - | -
Sample mean | $\bar x_\text{L}=170\text{ mg}$ | $\bar x_\text{D}=164\text{ mg}$
Sample standard deviation | $s_\text{L}=5.1\text{ mg}$ | $s_\text{D}=3.1\text{ mg}$
Sample size | $n_\text{L}=25$ |$n_\text{D}=27$

Shelby wants to estimate the difference in the mean caffeine content of a large coffee of the two types $\text{(light}-\text{dark)}$. Assume that the conditions for inference have been met.

**Which of the following is an appropriate confidence interval for $\mu_\text{L}-\mu_\text{D}$?**

[[â˜ƒ radio 1]]
hints:
###Components of formula

|| Caffeine contents of large coffees ||
 | Light roast | Dark roast
- | - | -
Sample mean | $\bar x_\text{L}=\greenD{170}\text{ mg}$ | $\bar x_\text{D}=\blueD{164}\text{ mg}$
Sample standard deviation | $s_\text{L}=\goldE{5.1}\text{ mg}$ | $s_\text{D}=\purpleC{3.1}\text{ mg}$
Sample size | $n_\text{L}=\maroonD{25}$ |$n_\text{D}=\redD{27}$

Note that Shelby wants a confidence interval for $\mu_\text{L}-\mu_\text{D}$:

$(\greenD{170}-\blueD{164}) \pm t^* \sqrt{\dfrac{\goldE{5.1}^2}{\maroonD{25}}+\dfrac{\purpleC{3.1}^2}{\redD{27}}}$

###Answer

An appropriate confidence interval for $\mu_\text{L}-\mu_\text{D}$ is:

$6 \pm t^* \sqrt{\dfrac{5.1^2}{25}+\dfrac{3.1^2}{27}}$

assessment_items.question_content:
Kylie suspected that when people exercise longer, their body temperatures change. She randomly assigned people to exercise for $30$ or $60$ minutes, then measured their temperatures. The $18$ people who exercised for $30$ minutes had a mean temperature of $\bar x_{30}=38.3^\circ\,\text{C}$ with a standard deviation $s_{30}=0.27^\circ\,\text{C}$. The $24$ people who exercised $60$ minutes had a mean temperature of $\bar x_{60}=38.9^\circ\,\text{C}$ with a standard deviation $s_{60}=0.29^\circ\,\text{C}$.

Kylie wants to estimate the difference in the mean body temperature after exercising for the two amounts of time $(30\, \text{min.}-60\,\text{min.})$. Assume that the conditions for inference have been met.

**Which of the following is an appropriate confidence interval for $\mu_{30}-\mu_{60}$?**

[[â˜ƒ radio 1]]
hints:
###Components of formula

|| Body temperatures after exercise ||
 | $30$ min. | $60$ min.
- | - | -
Sample mean | $\bar x_{30}=\greenD{38.3}^\circ\,\text{C}$ | $\bar x_{60}=\blueD{38.9}^\circ\,\text{C}$
Sample standard deviation | $s_{30}=\goldE{0.27}^\circ\,\text{C}$ | $s_\text{60}=\purpleC{0.29}^\circ\,\text{C}$
Sample size | $n_{30}=\maroonD{18}$ |$n_{60}=\redD{24}$

Note that Kylie wants a confidence interval for $\mu_{30}-\mu_{60}$:

$(\greenD{38.3}-\blueD{38.9}) \pm t^* \sqrt{\dfrac{\goldE{0.27}^2}{\maroonD{18}}+\dfrac{\purpleC{0.29}^2}{\redD{24}}}$

###Answer

An appropriate confidence interval for $\mu_{30}-\mu_{60}$ is:

$-0.6\pm t^* \sqrt{\dfrac{0.27^2}{18}+\dfrac{0.29^2}{24}}$

assessment_items.question_content:
Linn wanted to determine how much of a difference it makes to use a certain product to treat the water she used to irrigate her corn crops. She used a randomized block design experiment to select parts of her land to irrigate with untreated lake water and others to irrigate with treated water. Then she measured the heights of the plants in those areas. Here are summary statistics for her experiment:

|| Average plant height per section ||
 | Lake water | Treated water
- | - | -
Sample mean | $\bar x_\text{L}=276\text{ cm}$ | $\bar x_\text{T}=337\text{ cm}$
Sample standard deviation | $s_\text{L}=22\text{ cm}$ | $s_\text{T}=35\text{ cm}$
Sample size | $n_\text{L}=4$ |$n_\text{T}=4$

Linn wants to estimate the difference in the mean height (in centimeters) of the plants irrigated with each type of water $\text{(treated}-\text{lake)}$. Assume that the conditions for inference have been met.

**Which of the following is an appropriate confidence interval for $\mu_\text{T}-\mu_\text{L}$?**

[[â˜ƒ radio 1]]
hints:
###Components of formula

|| Average plant height per section ||
 | Lake water | Treated water
- | - | -
Sample mean | $\bar x_\text{L}=\greenD{276}\text{ cm}$ | $\bar x_\text{T}=\blueD{337}\text{ cm}$
Sample standard deviation | $s_\text{L}=\goldE{22}\text{ cm}$ | $s_\text{T}=\purpleC{35}\text{ cm}$
Sample size | $n_\text{L}=\maroonD{4}$ |$n_\text{T}=\redD{4}$

Note that Linn wants a confidence interval for $\mu_\text{T}-\mu_\text{L}$:

$(\blueD{337}-\greenD{276}) \pm t^* \sqrt{\dfrac{\purpleC{35}^2}{\redD{4}}+\dfrac{\goldE{22}^2}{\maroonD{4}}}$

###Answer

An appropriate confidence interval for $\mu_\text{T}-\mu_\text{L}$ is:

$61 \pm t^* \sqrt{\dfrac{35^2}{4}+\dfrac{22^2}{4}}$

assessment_items.question_content:
Having more cars running on a roller coaster shortens wait times but slows the ride. The owners asked two samples of $24$ customers to rate how exciting the roller coaster was on a scale from $1$ to $7$. The sample who rode the coaster when there were $4$ cars gave the coaster an average rating of $5.7$ points with a standard deviation of $0.6$ points. The sample who rode when there were $3$ cars gave an average rating of $6.1$ points with a standard deviation of $0.5$ points.

The owners wants to estimate the difference in the mean excitement ratings for the different numbers of cars on the roller coaster $(4\, \text{cars}-3\,\text{cars})$. Assume that the conditions for inference have been met.

**Which of the following is an appropriate confidence interval for $\mu_{4}-\mu_{3}$?**

[[â˜ƒ radio 1]]
hints:
###Components of formula

|| Excitement ratings ||
 | $4$ cars | $3$ cars
- | - | -
Sample mean | $\bar x_{4}=\greenD{5.7}$ | $\bar x_{3}=\blueD{6.1}$
Sample standard deviation | $s_{4}=\goldE{0.6}$ | $s_\text{3}=\purpleC{0.5}$
Sample size | $n_{4}=\maroonD{24}$ |$n_{3}=\redD{24}$

Note that the owners want a confidence interval for $\mu_{4}-\mu_{3}$:

$(\greenD{5.7}-\blueD{6.1}) \pm t^* \sqrt{\dfrac{\goldE{0.6}^2}{\maroonD{24}}+\dfrac{\purpleC{0.5}^2}{\redD{24}}}$

###Answer

An appropriate confidence interval for $\mu_{4}-\mu_{3}$ is:

$-0.4\pm t^* \sqrt{\dfrac{0.6^2}{24}+\dfrac{0.5^2}{24}}$

assessment_items.question_content:
Fast food companies want their products to be as similar as possible across locations. Miguel is tasked with making sure that two locations of a fast food chain have burger patties of the same size. He takes a random sample of $30$ patties from each location. Here are summary statistics for his samples:

|| Mass of burger patties ||
 | Location A | Location B
- | - | -
Sample mean | $\bar x_\text{A}=115\text{ g}$ | $\bar x_\text{B}=117\text{ g}$
Sample standard deviation | $s_\text{A}=2.8\text{ g}$ | $s_\text{B}=2.1\text{ g}$
Sample size | $n_\text{A}=30$ |$n_\text{B}=30$

Assume that the conditions for inference have been met.

**Which of the following is a $90\%$ confidence interval for the difference in mean weight (in grams) of the patties from the two restaurants $(\mu_\text{A}-\mu_\text{B})$?**  
*Use a calculator with statistical capabilities to calculate the interval.*

[[â˜ƒ radio 1]]
hints:
###Formula and strategy

We can make a confidence interval for the difference of two means like this:

$\begin{align}
(\text{sample difference}) &\pm (\text{critical value})\left(\overset{\text{standard error}}{\text{of difference}}\right) \\\\
(\bar{x}_1-\bar{x}_2)&\pm t^*\sqrt{\dfrac{s_1^2}{n_1}+\dfrac{s_2^2}{n_2}} 
\end{align}$

The degrees of freedom formula to find $t^*$ is pretty complex, so we use technology to calculate two-sample $t$ intervals like this. [[â˜ƒ explanation 1]]

Graphing calculators have the functionality to construct two-sample $t$ intervals. If you have your own graphing calculator and are not sure how to use it, you can search for help on the web. Different models work in different ways.

If you do not have your own calculator, you can use this one at [Stapplet.com](https://www.stapplet.com/quant1v_multi.html) (be sure to change the input to "Mean and standard deviation").

###Calculator entries

Here are the summary statistics that we enter on a graphing calculator. Note that Miguel wants a confidence interval for $\mu_\text{A}-\mu_\text{B}$, so we say location A is group $1$ and location B is group $2$:

|| **2-sample t interval** ||
 | $1$ | $2$
- | -: | -:
$\bar{x}$ | $115$ | $117$
$s$ | $2.8$ | $2.1$
$n$| $30$ | $30$

* Confidence level: $0.90$
* Pooled: No

Say "No" for "Pooled" in intervals like these. Pooled calculations assume equal variances in our two populations, and we have no reason to assume that.

###Answer

|| **Calculator output** ||
Title | 2-sample t interval
- | -: 
Lower bound | $-3.069$
Upper bound | $-0.931$
$\bar{x}$ difference | $-2$
margin of error | $1.070$
df | $53.783$

Miguel's $90\%$ confidence interval is:

$-2 \pm 1.07$, or written differently, $(-3.07,-0.93)$.

assessment_items.question_content:
A hospital uses magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) readings to take lung volume measurements of fetuses during pregnancy. They time how long each measurement takes. A sample of $9$ measurements with fetuses that are $21$ weeks old has a mean of $48$ seconds and a standard deviation of $8$ seconds. A sample of $9$ measurements with fetuses that are $29$ weeks old have a mean of $78$ seconds and a standard deviation of $13$ seconds. Assume that the conditions for inference have been met.

Let $\mu_{29}-\mu_{21}$ be the the difference in mean time required for the MRI for the different numbers of weeks into the pregnancy.

**Which of the following is a $99\%$ confidence interval for 
 $\mu_{29}-\mu_{21}$?**  
*Use a calculator with statistical capabilities to calculate the interval.*

[[â˜ƒ radio 1]]
hints:
###Formula and strategy

We can make a confidence interval for the difference of two means like this:

$\begin{align}
(\text{sample difference}) &\pm (\text{critical value})\left(\overset{\text{standard error}}{\text{of difference}}\right) \\\\
(\bar{x}_1-\bar{x}_2)&\pm t^*\sqrt{\dfrac{s_1^2}{n_1}+\dfrac{s_2^2}{n_2}} 
\end{align}$

The degrees of freedom formula to find $t^*$ is pretty complex, so we use technology to calculate two-sample $t$ intervals like this. [[â˜ƒ explanation 1]]

Graphing calculators have the functionality to construct two-sample $t$ intervals. If you have your own graphing calculator and are not sure how to use it, you can search for help on the web. Different models work in different ways.

If you do not have your own calculator, you can use this one at [Stapplet.com](https://www.stapplet.com/quant1v_multi.html) (be sure to change the input to "Mean and standard deviation").

###Calculator entries

Here are the summary statistics that we enter on a graphing calculator. Note that we want a confidence interval for $\mu_\text{29}-\mu_\text{21}$, so we say $29$ weeks is group $1$ and $21$ weeks is group $2$:

|| **2-sample t interval** ||
 | $1$ | $2$
- | -: | -:
$\bar{x}$ | $78$ | $48$
$s$ | $13$ | $8$
$n$| $9$ | $9$

* Confidence level: $0.99$
* Pooled: No

Say "No" for "Pooled" in intervals like these. Pooled calculations assume equal variances in our two populations, and we have no reason to assume that.

###Answer

|| **Calculator output** ||
Title | 2-sample t interval
- | -: 
Lower bound | $14.73$
Upper bound | $45.27$
$\bar{x}$ difference | $30$
margin of error | $15.27$
df | $13.299$

Our $99\%$ confidence interval is:

$30 \pm 15.3$, or written differently, $(14.7,45.3)$.

assessment_items.question_content:
Anouk conducted an experiment to see what difference plowing fields had on their total yield of crops.  The sample of $6$ fields that he plowed had a mean yield of $\bar x_{\text{P}}=11{,}700$ kilograms per hectare $\left(\dfrac{\text{kg}}{\text{ha}}\right)$ and a standard deviation of $4{,}900\,\dfrac{\text{kg}}{\text{ha}}$. The $7$ fields he didn't plow had a mean yield of $\bar x_{\text{N}}=9{,}620\,\dfrac{\text{kg}}{\text{ha}}$ and a standard deviation of $3{,}200\,\dfrac{\text{kg}}{\text{ha}}$.

Assume that the conditions for inference have been met.

**Which of the following is a $95\%$ confidence interval for the difference in mean yield (in kilograms per hectare) of the plowed and not plowed fields $(\mu_\text{P}-\mu_\text{N})$?**  
*Use a calculator with statistical capabilities to calculate the interval.*

[[â˜ƒ radio 1]]
hints:
###Formula and strategy

We can make a confidence interval for the difference of two means like this:

$\begin{align}
(\text{sample difference}) &\pm (\text{critical value})\left(\overset{\text{standard error}}{\text{of difference}}\right) \\\\
(\bar{x}_1-\bar{x}_2)&\pm t^*\sqrt{\dfrac{s_1^2}{n_1}+\dfrac{s_2^2}{n_2}} 
\end{align}$

The degrees of freedom formula to find $t^*$ is pretty complex, so we use technology to calculate two-sample $t$ intervals like this. [[â˜ƒ explanation 1]]

Graphing calculators have the functionality to construct two-sample $t$ intervals. If you have your own graphing calculator and are not sure how to use it, you can search for help on the web. Different models work in different ways.

If you do not have your own calculator, you can use this one at [Stapplet.com](https://www.stapplet.com/quant1v_multi.html) (be sure to change the input to "Mean and standard deviation").

###Calculator entries

Here are the summary statistics that we enter on a graphing calculator. Note that Anouk wants a confidence interval for $\mu_\text{P}-\mu_\text{N}$, so we say "plowed" is group $1$ and "not" is group $2$:

|| **2-sample t interval** ||
 | $1$ | $2$
- | -: | -:
$\bar{x}$ | $11700$ | $9620$
$s$ | $4900$ | $3200$
$n$| $6$ | $7$

* Confidence level: $0.95$
* Pooled: No

Say "No" for "Pooled" in intervals like these. Pooled calculations assume equal variances in our two populations, and we have no reason to assume that.

###Answer

|| **Calculator output** ||
Title | 2-sample t interval
- | -: 
Lower bound | $-3267$
Upper bound | $7427$
$\bar{x}$ difference | $2080$
margin of error | $5347$
df | $8.39$

Anouk's $95\%$ confidence interval is:

$2080 \pm 5347$, or written differently, $(-3267, 7427)$.

assessment_items.question_content:
A [[â˜ƒ definition 1]] measured the cholesterol levels of a sample of $25$ people between the ages of $35$ and $44$ years old. Here are summary statistics for the samples:

|| Cholesterol levels $(\text{mg per }100\text{mL})$  ||
 | Women $35$-$44$ years old | Men $35$-$44$ years old
- | - | -
Sample mean | $\bar x_\text{W}=213.6$ | $\bar x_\text{M}=226.8$
Sample standard deviation | $s_\text{W}=45.3$ | $s_\text{M}=49.4$
Sample size | $n_\text{W}=14$ |$n_\text{M}=11$

Assume that the conditions for inference have been met.

Let $\mu_\text{W}-\mu_\text{M}$ be the difference in mean cholesterol levels (in milligrams per $100$ milliliters) in women and men of those ages.

**Which of the following is a $90\%$ confidence interval for $\mu_\text{W}-\mu_\text{M}$?**  
*Use a calculator with statistical capabilities to calculate the interval.*

[[â˜ƒ radio 1]]
hints:
###Formula and strategy

We can make a confidence interval for the difference of two means like this:

$\begin{align}
(\text{sample difference}) &\pm (\text{critical value})\left(\overset{\text{standard error}}{\text{of difference}}\right) \\\\
(\bar{x}_1-\bar{x}_2)&\pm t^*\sqrt{\dfrac{s_1^2}{n_1}+\dfrac{s_2^2}{n_2}} 
\end{align}$

The degrees of freedom formula to find $t^*$ is pretty complex, so we use technology to calculate two-sample $t$ intervals like this. [[â˜ƒ explanation 1]]

Graphing calculators have the functionality to construct two-sample $t$ intervals. If you have your own graphing calculator and are not sure how to use it, you can search for help on the web. Different models work in different ways.

If you do not have your own calculator, you can use this one at [Stapplet.com](https://www.stapplet.com/quant1v_multi.html) (be sure to change the input to "Mean and standard deviation").

###Calculator entries

Here are the summary statistics that we enter on a graphing calculator. Note that we want a confidence interval for $\mu_\text{W}-\mu_\text{M}$, so we say women are group $1$ and men are group $2$:

|| **2-sample t interval** ||
 | $1$ | $2$
- | -: | -:
$\bar{x}$ | $213.6$ | $226.8$
$s$ | $45.3$ | $49.4$
$n$| $14$ | $11$

* Confidence level: $0.90$
* Pooled: No

Say "No" for "Pooled" in intervals like these. Pooled calculations assume equal variances in our two populations, and we have no reason to assume that.

###Answer

|| **Calculator output** ||
Title | 2-sample t interval
- | -: 
Lower bound | $-46.3$
Upper bound | $19.9$
$\bar{x}$ difference | $-13.2$
margin of error | $33.1$
df | $20.646$

Our $90\%$ confidence interval is:

$-13.2 \pm 33.1$, or written differently, $(-46.3,19.9)$.

options.definition: a professional who removes blood from a person for medical reasons

assessment_items.question_content:
Sophia tested two versions of a program on her website, one with badges for user accomplishments and one without. The sample of users exposed to the version with badges spent an average of $12$ minutes on the site during the testing month with a standard deviation of $26$ minutes. The sample who didn't have badges spent an average of $8$ minutes with a standard deviation of $22$ minutes during the month. There were $30$ users in each sample.

Assume that the conditions for inference have been met.

Let $\mu_\text{B}-\mu_\text{N}$ be the difference in mean time (in minutes) users spent on the website for the version with badges and the version without badges.

**Which of the following is a $95\%$ confidence interval for $\mu_\text{B}-\mu_\text{N}$?**  
*Use a calculator with statistical capabilities to calculate the interval.*

[[â˜ƒ radio 1]]
hints:
###Formula and strategy

We can make a confidence interval for the difference of two means like this:

$\begin{align}
(\text{sample difference}) &\pm (\text{critical value})\left(\overset{\text{standard error}}{\text{of difference}}\right) \\\\
(\bar{x}_1-\bar{x}_2)&\pm t^*\sqrt{\dfrac{s_1^2}{n_1}+\dfrac{s_2^2}{n_2}} 
\end{align}$

The degrees of freedom formula to find $t^*$ is pretty complex, so we use technology to calculate two-sample $t$ intervals like this. [[â˜ƒ explanation 1]]

Graphing calculators have the functionality to construct two-sample $t$ intervals. If you have your own graphing calculator and are not sure how to use it, you can search for help on the web. Different models work in different ways.

If you do not have your own calculator, you can use this one at [Stapplet.com](https://www.stapplet.com/quant1v_multi.html) (be sure to change the input to "Mean and standard deviation").

###Calculator entries

Here are the summary statistics that we enter on a graphing calculator. Note that Sophia wants a confidence interval for $\mu_\text{B}-\mu_\text{N}$, so we say "badges" is group $1$ and "without" is group $2$:

|| **2-sample t interval** ||
 | $1$ | $2$
- | -: | -:
$\bar{x}$ | $12$ | $8$
$s$ | $26$ | $22$
$n$| $30$ | $30$

* Confidence level: $0.95$
* Pooled: No

Say "No" for "Pooled" in intervals like these. Pooled calculations assume equal variances in our two populations, and we have no reason to assume that.

###Answer

|| **Calculator output** ||
Title | 2-sample t interval
- | -: 
Lower bound | $-8.454$
Upper bound | $16.454$
$\bar{x}$ difference | $4$
margin of error | $12.454$
df | $56.453$

Sophia's $95\%$ confidence interval is:

$(-8.5,16.5)$, or written differently, $4 \pm 12.5$.

assessment_items.question_content:
Esteban suspects that right-handed participants have an unfair advantage on a memory test because the input device uses the right hand. He selects a random sample of existing test data and summarizes it in the following table.

|| Scores on memory test  (points) ||
 | Left-handed | Right-handed
- | - | -
Sample mean | $\bar x_\text{L}=115.3$ | $\bar x_\text{R}=116.8$
Sample standard deviation | $s_\text{L}=26.2$ | $s_\text{R}=15.8$
Sample size | $n_\text{L}=9$ |$n_\text{R}=24$

Assume that the conditions for inference have been met.

Let $\mu_\text{R}-\mu_\text{L}$ be the difference in mean score of the right- and left-handed participants.

**Which of the following is a $90\%$ confidence interval for $\mu_\text{R}-\mu_\text{L}$?**  
*Use a calculator with statistical capabilities to calculate the interval.*

[[â˜ƒ radio 1]]
hints:
###Formula and strategy

We can make a confidence interval for the difference of two means like this:

$\begin{align}
(\text{sample difference}) &\pm (\text{critical value})\left(\overset{\text{standard error}}{\text{of difference}}\right) \\\\
(\bar{x}_1-\bar{x}_2)&\pm t^*\sqrt{\dfrac{s_1^2}{n_1}+\dfrac{s_2^2}{n_2}} 
\end{align}$

The degrees of freedom formula to find $t^*$ is pretty complex, so we use technology to calculate two-sample $t$ intervals like this. [[â˜ƒ explanation 1]]

Graphing calculators have the functionality to construct two-sample $t$ intervals. If you have your own graphing calculator and are not sure how to use it, you can search for help on the web. Different models work in different ways.

If you do not have your own calculator, you can use this one at [Stapplet.com](https://www.stapplet.com/quant1v_multi.html) (be sure to change the input to "Mean and standard deviation").

###Calculator entries

Here are the summary statistics that we enter on a graphing calculator. Note that Esteban wants a confidence interval for $\mu_\text{R}-\mu_\text{L}$, so we say right-handed is group $1$ and left-handed is group $2$:

|| **2-sample t interval** ||
 | $1$ | $2$
- | -: | -:
$\bar{x}$ | $116.8$ | $115.3$
$s$ | $15.8$ | $26.2$
$n$| $24$ | $9$

* Confidence level: $0.90$
* Pooled: No

Say "No" for "Pooled" in intervals like these. Pooled calculations assume equal variances in our two populations, and we have no reason to assume that.

###Answer

|| **Calculator output** ||
Title | 2-sample t interval
- | -: 
Lower bound | $-15.33$
Upper bound | $18.33$
$\bar{x}$ difference | $1.5$
margin of error | $16.83$
df | $10.264$

Esteban's $90\%$ confidence interval is:

$(-15.33,18.33)$, or written differently, $1.5 \pm 16.83$.

assessment_items.question_content:
Chana surveyed students from her school about how they travel to school and how long it takes them. The following tables summarizes her results.

|| Travel time to school ||
 | Cycling | Walking
- | - | -
Sample mean | $\bar x_\text{C}=23.0\text{ min}$ | $\bar x_\text{W}=20.4\text{ min}$
Sample standard deviation | $s_\text{C}=5.9\text{ min}$ | $s_\text{W}=6.6\text{ min}$
Sample size | $n_\text{C}=4$ |$n_\text{W}=8$

Assume that the conditions for inference have been met.

**Which of the following is a $99\%$ confidence interval for the difference in mean travel time (in minutes) for the different transportation methods $(\mu_\text{C}-\mu_\text{W})$?**  
*Use a calculator with statistical capabilities to calculate the interval.*

[[â˜ƒ radio 1]]
hints:
###Formula and strategy

We can make a confidence interval for the difference of two means like this:

$\begin{align}
(\text{sample difference}) &\pm (\text{critical value})\left(\overset{\text{standard error}}{\text{of difference}}\right) \\\\
(\bar{x}_1-\bar{x}_2)&\pm t^*\sqrt{\dfrac{s_1^2}{n_1}+\dfrac{s_2^2}{n_2}} 
\end{align}$

The degrees of freedom formula to find $t^*$ is pretty complex, so we use technology to calculate two-sample $t$ intervals like this. [[â˜ƒ explanation 1]]

Graphing calculators have the functionality to construct two-sample $t$ intervals. If you have your own graphing calculator and are not sure how to use it, you can search for help on the web. Different models work in different ways.

If you do not have your own calculator, you can use this one at [Stapplet.com](https://www.stapplet.com/quant1v_multi.html) (be sure to change the input to "Mean and standard deviation").

###Calculator entries

Here are the summary statistics that we enter on a graphing calculator. Note that Chana wants a confidence interval for $\mu_\text{C}-\mu_\text{W}$, so we say cycling is group $1$ and walking is group $2$:

|| **2-sample t interval** ||
 | $1$ | $2$
- | -: | -:
$\bar{x}$ | $23$ | $20.4$
$s$ | $5.9$ | $6.6$
$n$| $4$ | $8$

* Confidence level: $0.99$
* Pooled: No

Say "No" for "Pooled" in intervals like these. Pooled calculations assume equal variances in our two populations, and we have no reason to assume that.

###Answer

|| **Calculator output** ||
Title | 2-sample t interval
- | -: 
Lower bound | $-10.7$
Upper bound | $15.9$
$\bar{x}$ difference | $2.6$
margin of error | $13.3$
df | $6.789$

Chana's $99\%$ confidence interval is:

$(-10.7,15.9)$, or written differently, $2.6 \pm 13.3$.

assessment_items.question_content:
Tyrone is an investment broker who tells his clients that some investments are a moderate risk and others are an aggressive risk. He wants to know the difference between the annual return on the investments in each category. He sampled $17$ investments from each category. There was a mean annual return of $6.2\%$ on the investments in the moderate sample, with a standard deviation of $9.3\%$. The aggressive investments had a sample mean of $7.3\%$ and a standard deviation of $12.4\%$.

Assume that the conditions for inference have been met.

Let $\mu_\text{A}-\mu_\text{M}$ be the difference in mean annual return (in percents) of the groups of investments $(\text{aggressive}-\text{moderate})$.

**Which of the following is a $95\%$ confidence interval for $\mu_\text{A}-\mu_\text{M}$?**  
*Use a calculator with statistical capabilities to calculate the interval.*

[[â˜ƒ radio 1]]
hints:
###Formula and strategy

We can make a confidence interval for the difference of two means like this:

$\begin{align}
(\text{sample difference}) &\pm (\text{critical value})\left(\overset{\text{standard error}}{\text{of difference}}\right) \\\\
(\bar{x}_1-\bar{x}_2)&\pm t^*\sqrt{\dfrac{s_1^2}{n_1}+\dfrac{s_2^2}{n_2}} 
\end{align}$

The degrees of freedom formula to find $t^*$ is pretty complex, so we use technology to calculate two-sample $t$ intervals like this. [[â˜ƒ explanation 1]]

Graphing calculators have the functionality to construct two-sample $t$ intervals. If you have your own graphing calculator and are not sure how to use it, you can search for help on the web. Different models work in different ways.

If you do not have your own calculator, you can use this one at [Stapplet.com](https://www.stapplet.com/quant1v_multi.html) (be sure to change the input to "Mean and standard deviation").

###Calculator entries

Here are the summary statistics that we enter on a graphing calculator. Note that Tyrone wants a confidence interval for $\mu_\text{A}-\mu_\text{M}$, so we say "aggressive" is group $1$ and "moderate" is group $2$:

|| **2-sample t interval** ||
 | $1$ | $2$
- | -: | -:
$\bar{x}$ | $7.3$ | $6.2$
$s$ | $12.4$ | $9.3$
$n$| $17$ | $17$

* Confidence level: $0.95$
* Pooled: No

Say "No" for "Pooled" in intervals like these. Pooled calculations assume equal variances in our two populations, and we have no reason to assume that.

###Answer

|| **Calculator output** ||
Title | 2-sample t interval
- | -: 
Lower bound | $-6.6$
Upper bound | $8.8$
$\bar{x}$ difference | $1.1$
margin of error | $7.7$
df | $29.674$

Tyrone's $95\%$ confidence interval is:

$(-6.6,8.8)$, or written differently, $1.1 \pm 7.7$.
